fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need a small utility library that handles all the annoying low-level plumbing stuff our CLI tool keeps reinventing. Basically things like expanding those dollar-sign placeholder tokens in config strings, parsing those semicolon-separated key=value blobs users pass as single arguments (remember how messy that got last time with the quoted connection strings?), figuring out relative paths between project directories, reading properties out of .csproj files, picking the right SDK version from the installed list, and sorting out the Linux/macOS user identity stuff. The user-identity piece especially needs to be testable without actually running OS commands — similar to how we handled the injectable abstraction in that other auth probe module we did. For the key=value parsing, there was a specific edge case that caused a support ticket a while back around empty keys — make sure that's handled gracefully, not just a crash. The path logic also needs to handle Windows-style backslash paths coming in even when running on Linux. Output should just be simple line-based text. The whole thing should be split into sensible groupings of related functionality rather than one giant file. We want to be able to drop this in and have the CLI call these helpers without them knowing anything about JSON or stdin/stdout.

trigger_keywords: what happens with an unmatched placeholder token
what if the environment variable is missing
behavior when substitution variable not found
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A $(NAME) token whose name has no matching environment entry must be left exactly as written in the output, including the surrounding $( and ) characters. It is not replaced with an empty string and does not produce an error. The output line format is: result=<expanded string>

trigger_keywords: empty key in key value pair
what to return when key is blank
how to handle =aaa style input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A key/value pair where the key is empty (e.g. input '=aaa') is a domain error. The output must be exactly the single line: error=command_line_parse_error\n — nothing else, no count line, no partial results. This must never surface as an unhandled exception.

trigger_keywords: quoted values in key value parsing
how are double quotes handled in option string
can a value contain equals or semicolon
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Either or both sides of a key=value pair may be wrapped in double quotes. The quotes are stripped from the result. Quoting allows a value to contain literal '=' and ';' characters. For example: "ConnectionString"="User=foo;Password=test" parses to a single pair with key ConnectionString and value User=foo;Password=test.

trigger_keywords: trailing semicolon in option string
what if option string ends with semicolon
empty pair at end of semicolon list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A trailing semicolon at the end of an option string is tolerated and does not produce an extra empty pair. For example 'ShouldCreateTable=true;BlogTableName=;' is treated the same as 'ShouldCreateTable=true;BlogTableName='.

trigger_keywords: backslash path on linux
windows path separator handling
case sensitivity in path comparison
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both backslashes and forward slashes are treated as path separators during normalization. Segment comparison is case-insensitive. The output always uses forward slashes as separators. A trailing separator on the target (relative_to) path is preserved in the result.

trigger_keywords: trailing slash preserved in relative path
does output keep the ending slash
relative path trailing separator
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the target path (relative_to) ends with a trailing separator, that trailing slash is preserved in the relative_path output. For example if relative_to is 'user/Solution/Project/' the output is relative_path=Project/ not relative_path=Project.

trigger_keywords: short switch vs long switch argument parsing
are -c and --configuration interchangeable
case sensitivity of argument switches
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both the short form (e.g. -c) and the long form (e.g. --configuration) of a declared option switch must be accepted interchangeably and produce identical output. Switch matching is case-insensitive. The token immediately following a matched switch is consumed as the value and coerced to the declared type (string, int, or bool). Boolean values render as lowercase true or false.

trigger_keywords: AOT override from build parameters
msbuild parameters take precedence over project file
publish aot resolution order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: AOT resolution uses this precedence: (1) if the msbuild_parameters string contains publishaot=true or publishaot=false (matched case-insensitively with whitespace ignored) that value wins and the project file is not consulted. (2) Otherwise the project file's PublishAot element is read; if present and a valid boolean its value is used. (3) If absent or not a valid boolean, the result defaults to false. Output format: publish_aot=true or publish_aot=false.

trigger_keywords: how is SDK version selected
which SDK version wins when multiple installed
sdk list parsing format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The SDK version listing is a multi-line string where each line starts with a version number followed by an install path in brackets (e.g. '2.1.403 [/usr/local/share/dotnet/sdk]'). The primitive parses all version numbers and returns the highest valid semantic version found. Output format: sdk_version=<version>.

trigger_keywords: user resolution failure handling
what happens when id command fails
posix user probe error output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a probe command fails (non-zero exit code), two lines are emitted before the resolution result: first a diagnostic line in the format 'Error executing "<command>" - exit code <code> <error_output>', then a warning line 'Warning: Unable to get effective group from "<command>"' (or user equivalent). The final line is 'user_resolved=false'. When both probes succeed the output is 'user_id=<uid>\ngroup_id=<gid>\n'.

trigger_keywords: injectable process probe for user resolution
how to test user id without running OS commands
abstraction for running id command in tests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The user-resolution primitive must depend on an injectable process-probe abstraction and an injectable logger abstraction rather than directly executing OS processes. This allows test doubles to supply scripted results (executed flag, exit_code, output string, error string) without spawning real processes. The interface must be kept small and focused per ISP.

trigger_keywords: code organization structure
how many files should the library have
what goes in which module or unit
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Related primitives should be grouped into cohesive units rather than one monolithic file or one file per trivial function. Suggested groupings: (1) string/option-parsing unit (env substitution, key-value parsing), (2) path/manifest unit (relative path, XML project manifest inspection), (3) process/user unit (SDK version selection, POSIX user resolution), (4) argument-parsing unit. The core primitives must know nothing about stdin/stdout or JSON — a thin execution adapter handles JSON decode and output formatting.

trigger_keywords: what does the adapter do with JSON
how is the output formatted
where does JSON parsing happen
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A thin execution adapter is solely responsible for: decoding the JSON input payload, calling the appropriate primitive with native values, and formatting the result as plain line-based text output. The core primitives take and return ordinary native values (strings, maps, booleans, numeric versions, small structs) and must never reference JSON, stdin, or stdout directly.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the injectable abstraction in that other auth probe module we did
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7 (POSIX user resolution) requires a dependency-injected process-probe interface and logger interface as described in the DIP constraint in the Architecture section of start.md, enabling test doubles via the id_results scripted input in feature7_posix_user.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: there was a specific edge case that caused a support ticket a while back around empty keys
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2 key/value parsing: a pair with an empty key (e.g. '=aaa') must output exactly 'error=command_line_parse_error\n' as specified in feature2_key_value_parsing.json and the Resilience NFR in start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: those dollar-sign placeholder tokens in config strings
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1 environment variable substitution: the $(NAME) token format defined in feature1_env_substitution.json, where unmatched tokens are left verbatim

context_pointers.vague_reference: those semicolon-separated key=value blobs users pass as single arguments (remember how messy that got last time with the quoted connection strings)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2 key/value parsing with double-quote support allowing values to contain literal '=' and ';', as shown in the ConnectionString test case in feature2_key_value_parsing.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — you'll need to look at the existing patterns in the codebase or check with the original implementer. The general principle is that these are small, well-defined primitives with predictable behavior on every edge case, so when in doubt err on the side of explicit error reporting rather than silent fallback.

feature1_env_substitution.json.description: Substitute $(NAME) tokens inside a string with values from the process environment. Tokens whose name has no matching environment entry are left untouched, and a string with no tokens passes through unchanged. The same token may appear multiple times and every occurrence is replaced.

feature2_key_value_parsing.json.description: Parse a semicolon-delimited list of key=value pairs into a map, reporting how many pairs were found followed by each key=value. Values may be quoted so they can themselves contain '=' and ';' characters; a trailing ';' is tolerated; an empty value is allowed. A pair with an empty key is rejected as a command-line parse error.

feature3_relative_path.json.description: Compute the relative path from a start location to a target location. Both inputs are normalized (backslashes treated as separators, comparison is case-insensitive); each directory left behind on the start side becomes a '../' segment and the remaining target segments are appended with '/'. When the start path is an ancestor of the target the result is just the descending segments; a trailing separator on the target is preserved.

feature4_argument_parsing.json.description: Parse a list of command-line tokens against a set of declared options. Each option declares a name, a short switch (single dash) and a long switch (double dash), and a value type (string, int, or bool). When a switch is matched, the following token is consumed and coerced to the declared type. The result reports how many options were matched and, for each matched option, its name, value type, and parsed value. Both the short and long switch forms are accepted interchangeably.

feature5_1_target_framework.json.description: Given the text of an XML project manifest, return the value of the project's target framework property. The manifest's SDK style and other properties vary, but the target framework is read regardless of the surrounding structure.

feature5_2_output_type.json.description: Given the text of an XML project manifest, return the value of the project's output type property (for example a library versus an executable).

feature5_3_publish_aot.json.description: Decide whether ahead-of-time (AOT) publishing is requested. An optional build-parameter string is consulted first: if it contains the publish-AOT flag set to true or false (case-insensitive, whitespace ignored) that value wins. Otherwise the project manifest's publish-AOT property is read; if it is absent or not a boolean the result defaults to false.

feature6_sdk_version.json.description: Parse the multi-line listing produced by an SDK-list command. Each line begins with a version number followed by an install path in brackets. Return the highest valid version found by scanning the listing.

feature7_posix_user.json.description: Resolve the effective user id and group id by running two probe commands in sequence (one for the user id, one for the group id). Each probe reports whether it executed, an exit code, captured output, and captured error. When both probes succeed and emit numeric output, the resolved user id and group id are returned. When a probe fails (non-zero exit), a diagnostic line describing the failed probe is recorded, a warning notes which value could not be obtained, and the user is reported as unresolved.

cases.expected_output:
Error executing "id -g" - exit code -1 Sad trombones
Warning: Unable to get effective group from "id -g"
user_resolved=false
